answer:
Ghalib (ghazal 1, 'Aa ke meri jaan ko qarar nahin hai') and Zauq (ghazal 1, 'Aankh us pur-jafa se ladti hai') both close with the takhallus, but in contrasting registers.

Ghalib's maqta: 'You've sworn an oath, O Ghalib, to give up the wine, / But in your vow, the faith of any prayer is not there.' (p-4304e1). He addresses himself in the second person to point out his own perjury — the takhallus is the punchline of his own ironic self-exposure. He neither praises nor humbles himself; he catches himself out.

Zauq's maqta: 'O Zauq, ''tis true that "war is but deceitful play," / And so her glance with pure and practiced guile contends.' (p-c251f5). The takhallus is the tag for a quoted aphorism — Zauq deploys his name as a frame for received wisdom, deferring to a known proverb rather than performing the closing wit himself. The stance is conventional, courtly, modest.

The contrast: Ghalib uses the maqta as a mirror that catches him in vice; Zauq uses it as a citation slot that hands the closing wit to tradition. Ghalib's irony is at his own expense; Zauq's humility is at the proverb's service.
